title: Living Hope and Holy Living
subtitle: Elect exiles born again to a living hope, called to holiness in reverent fear

sections.header: Verses 1-2 - "Greeting: Elect Exiles of the Dispersion"

heb.paragraph: The recipients are elect - chosen by God before the foundation of the world. Election is the theological ground of their identity as Christians.
They are exiles, resident aliens in this world. The term denotes temporary residence in a foreign land.
Originally used of Jews scattered among the nations, Peter applies it to Gentile Christians scattered across Asia Minor.

panels.ctx: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor. The greeting establishes their identity: elect according to God the Father, sanctified by the Spirit, destined for obedience and sprinkling with Christ the blood. The Trinitarian formula grounds their election in the work of the entire Godhead.

cross.note: He chose us in him before the foundation of the world.
You are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world.
They acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.
Moses sprinkled the blood of the covenant on the people.

notes.note: Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ - his authority is apostolic. The recipients are elect exiles, chosen by God yet strangers in this world.
Election is according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, in the sanctification of the Spirit, for obedience to Jesus Christ and sprinkling with his blood.
Calvin emphasizes that election precedes faith. God chose them before they believed; the Spirit sanctifies those the Father foreknew.
The term parepidemos (exiles) emphasizes their transient status in a hostile world. They are resident aliens whose true home is elsewhere.
Sprinkling with blood recalls the covenant inauguration at Sinai (Exodus 24:8) and the Day of Atonement.
Schreiner notes that the greeting establishes the theological foundation for the entire letter. Their identity as elect exiles determines how they should live in a hostile world.
Jobes emphasizes the social reality: these Christians are marginalized in Asia Minor society. Peter reframes their marginalization as divine election.

sections.header: Verses 3-12 - "Born Again to a Living Hope"

heb.gloss: to beget again, cause to be born again
heb.paragraph: God has caused us to be born again. The verb is divine passive - God is the agent of regeneration. New birth is not human achievement but divine gift.
A living hope - not wishful thinking but confident expectation grounded in resurrection. The hope is living because Christ lives.
The genuineness of your faith - more precious than gold that perishes though tested by fire. Trials prove faith genuine.

panels.ctx: Peter bursts into doxology: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! The blessing celebrates new birth through resurrection, a living hope, an imperishable inheritance kept in heaven. Present trials test faith genuineness, producing praise when Christ is revealed. The prophets searched about this salvation; angels long to look into it.

cross.note: Unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God.
We were buried with him by baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised, we too might walk in newness of life.
Count it all joy when you meet trials, for the testing of faith produces steadfastness.
All these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised.

notes.note: New birth comes through resurrection. The inheritance is imperishable, undefiled, unfading - kept in heaven for you who are being guarded by God.
In this you rejoice, though now for a little while you have been grieved by various trials. The testing of faith is more precious than gold.
The prophets searched and inquired about this salvation. They served not themselves but you. Angels long to look into these things.
Calvin notes that hope is living because it is grounded in resurrection - a dead hope comes from a dead savior. The inheritance is heavenly, not earthly.
Faith must be tested. As gold is refined by fire, so faith is purified by trial. The outcome is praise, glory, and honor at Christ revelation.
The phrase living hope uses a perfect participle - the hope is alive and remains alive because Christ rose and remains risen.
The comparative more precious than gold emphasizes value. Gold perishes despite testing; faith, though tested, yields eternal reward.
Angels long to look into these things - the verb parakupto means to bend down and peer intently. Even angels are curious about redemption.
Schreiner emphasizes that vv. 3-12 form one long sentence in Greek - a sustained blessing. The structure mirrors Ephesians 1:3-14. Everything flows from God mercy in regeneration.
Jobes notes the pastoral purpose: suffering readers need assurance that their trials are not meaningless. Peter grounds encouragement in eschatological hope.

sections.header: Verses 13-21 - "Be Holy, for I Am Holy"

heb.gloss: to gird up, prepare for action
heb.paragraph: Gird up the loins of your mind - a metaphor from tucking robes into a belt for work or travel. Mental preparation for action.
Be holy, for I am holy - a direct quotation from Leviticus. Holiness is not optional but obligatory; it flows from God character.
Ransomed not with perishable things like silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ. The ransom metaphor implies slavery and liberation.

panels.ctx: Peter moves from indicative (what God has done) to imperative (how to respond). The command is holiness - modeled on God own character (Leviticus 11:44-45; 19:2). The motivation is twofold: they are to live as strangers in reverent fear, knowing they will give account; they were ransomed from futile ways with Christ precious blood, foreknown before creation.

cross.note: Be holy, for I am holy - the command Peter quotes.
You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.
The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many.
In him we have redemption through his blood.

notes.note: Gird up your minds, be sober, set your hope fully on grace. As obedient children, do not conform to former passions. Be holy, for I am holy.
Conduct yourselves with fear - not terror but reverent awe. You were ransomed with precious blood, not corruptible silver or gold.
Calvin sees holiness as both gift and command. God makes holy; we must become what we are. The imperative flows from the indicative.
The ransom price demonstrates value. If God paid Christ blood, how precious must the redeemed be!
The phrase gird up the loins of your mind adapts OT imagery (Exodus 12:11; 1 Kings 18:46) to mental discipline.
Futile ways inherited from your fathers likely refers to pagan religious practices. The readers are Gentiles converted from idolatry.
Foreknown before the foundation of the world - Christ sacrifice was not Plan B but the eternal purpose of God.
Schreiner notes the logical progression: hope (v. 13) to holiness (vv. 14-16) to fear (v. 17) to redemption (vv. 18-21). Eschatology grounds ethics.
Jobes emphasizes that the readers former way of life was not neutral but futile - empty, vain, leading nowhere. Conversion is rescue from meaninglessness.

sections.header: Verses 22-25 - "Born Again Through the Living Word"

heb.paragraph: Having purified your souls by obedience to the truth - purification is accomplished, evidenced by genuine brotherly love.
Sincere brotherly love - love without pretense, without mask. The word derives from hypokrisis (acting, pretending).
Born again not of perishable seed but of imperishable - through the living and abiding word of God.

panels.ctx: Peter returns to new birth, now emphasizing its ethical implications. Purification by obedience leads to sincere love; love must be earnest, from a pure heart. The imperishable seed is the word of God - living and abiding, in contrast to human flesh which withers like grass. Isaiah 40:6-8 is quoted: the grass withers, the flower falls, but the word of the Lord remains forever.

cross.note: A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another.
The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever.
Of his own will he brought us forth by the word of truth.
No one born of God makes a practice of sinning, for God seed abides in him.

notes.note: Purified souls lead to sincere love, earnest love from a pure heart. New birth is by imperishable seed, the living and abiding word of God.
All flesh is like grass - withering, falling. But the word of the Lord remains forever. This word is the gospel preached to you.
Calvin stresses that love is the test of regeneration. Those born of imperishable seed will produce imperishable fruit - sincere, earnest love for the brethren.
The verb purified (hegnikos) is perfect tense - purification is accomplished and its effects continue. Obedience to truth is the means.
The contrast between perishable and imperishable seed distinguishes human generation from divine regeneration.
Schreiner notes that the section rounds off chapter 1 by returning to new birth (cf. v. 3). The instrument is now specified: the word of God, identified as the gospel.
Jobes emphasizes the community dimension: love is not abstract but directed toward brothers and sisters. Regeneration creates a new family with new obligations.

rows.label: vv. 3-12
rows.text: Doxology: living hope and tested faith

rows.label: vv. 13-21
rows.text: Imperative: be holy, you were ransomed

lit.note: Chapter 1 establishes identity (elect exiles), celebrates salvation (living hope), and commands holiness (be holy as God is holy).

themes.note: Holiness dominates as the explicit command. Sovereignty is strong: election, foreknowledge, guarding. Faith is tested and proven.
